Programs may be a single event, a short series, or continuously recurring. They may include but are not limited to: lectures, forums, visual and performance art, interactive classes or workshops, continuing education, fairs, discussion groups, technology programs, story times, class visits, library tours, community outreach, exhibits, and presentations for social, cultural, educational, or recreational purposes. Programs may be delivered by Library staff or Library partners. POLICY: Ultimate responsibility for programming rests with the Library Director who may take on management of particular programs but primarily delegates program management to the Head of Public Service, the Adult Services Librarian, the Teen Librarian, the Children’s Librarian, and to other staff as appropriate. Programming librarians are guided by this policy and utilize professional skills, collections, equipment, facilities, and feedback from the community in planning and delivering library programs. Programs are developed with the following criteria in mind: Relevance to APL’s mission and strategic plan Community needs and interests, including from feedback and suggestions of community members Presentation quality as determined by the presenter’s background, record of presentation, and qualifications in content area Historical, cultural, and/or educational significance Balance of current programs being offered at the Library Connection to other community programs, exhibitions, or events National and local observances, commemorative months, and initiatives (e.g. National Library Week, Women’s History Month, Veteran’s Day, etc.) Space required and the availability of that space Budgetary considerations Staff time Any presenter interested in having a program at APL may submit a proposal that will be reviewed according to the above criteria. If you would like to host a program co-sponsored by the library that would be open and of educational, cultural or civic interest to the general public, please contact the Library Director by filling out this application form to inquire about the possibility of co-sponsoring the event with the Library. APL retains the right to decline a program proposal for several reasons, including but not limited to staffing issues, budget limitations, space availability, scheduling conflicts, or if the proposal does not align with the previously mentioned criteria. APL’s philosophy of open access to information and ideas extends to its programming and sponsorship of a program does not constitute an endorsement of the content or the views expressed by participants. All programs will be open to the public. In certain cases, the Abbot Public Library Foundation and the Friends of Abbot Public Library may charge fees for admittance and collect donations. Programs may be held on-site at APL, off-site at various community locations, and/or online. Presenters must comply with all APL policies. Programs may be designed for and limited to a specific audience, age, or grade level: Children are defined as those that are ages 0 through 12 years. Teens are defined as those that are ages 13 through 18 years. Adults are defined as those that are 19 years and older. In keeping with the ALA’s Bill of Rights–Access to Resources and Services to Minors, parents/guardians, not APL, are responsible for monitoring attendance of their children at APL programs. Registration may be required for a program in order to: Not exceed the capacity of the venue. Ensure the audience meets all requirements. Allow the presenter to adequately prepare for the number of participants. In the event that a program is in high demand and the registration list is full, a waitlist may be created. Should spots in the program become available, APL will make every reasonable effort to notify waitlisted patrons in time for them to attend the program. In the event that there is no waitlist, walk-ins may be allowed to fill unclaimed spots. Authors, artists, and entertainers may sell books, artwork and/or other materials as part of an APL program. For book sales, an arrangement with a local bookstore is preferred . No presenter shall collect names for solicitation or promotional purposes. APL marketing including flyers, event listings on the Library website and social media will occur as part of hosting a program. Upon request, presenters will receive links to the library online calendar of events and a flyer designed by the Library. Individuals, groups, and organizations reserving spaces within APL for meetings or events not co-hosted with the Library are responsible for their own marketing. These meetings/events are not promoted by APL. For more information regarding how to reserve Library space for these kinds of programs, please refer to our Meeting Room Policy . Attendees must behave in accordance with APL’s Code of Conduct Policy . Programs may be videotaped and/or photographed. Programs may be canceled for several reasons including but not limited to severe weather, absence of the presenter, low registration, etc. Canceled programs are not automatically rescheduled. When possible, advanced notification of cancellations will be made on APL’s online Calendar of Events and by other appropriate communication means. PROCESS FOR RECONSIDERATION: The decision to participate in library programs is a personal one. While an individual may choose not to attend a program for themselves, they cannot impose censorship to limit access for others. Recognizing that a diversity of events may result in some requests for reconsideration, the following procedures have been developed to ensure that objections or complaints are handled in an attentive and consistent manner. Concerns about programs currently offered at APL may be expressed by filling out a Request for Reconsideration form which can be received from the Library Director. Completed forms may be submitted in writing to the attention of the Library Director either in person or ℅ Abbot Public Library, 235 Pleasant Street, Marblehead, MA 01945. Those submitting a Request for Reconsideration will be given copies of APL’s Programming Policy as well as copies of the ALA Bill of Rights, the Freedom to Read, and the Freedom to View. Anonymous submissions will not be considered, nor will submissions from individuals or stakeholders not residing or located in Marblehead, MA. A Request for Reconsideration will be reviewed by the Library Director, who will provide a written response to the person initiating the request. That individual may choose to appeal the decision to the Library Board of Trustees, whose decision will be final. Throughout the reconsideration process, the program will remain as scheduled. Approved by the Board of Trustees, January 2025. Security Camera Policy Security Camera Policy 1. Purpose Marblehead’s Abbot Public Library (the “Library”) strives to maintain a safe and secure environment for its patrons, staff, and property while, at the same time, protecting individual privacy rights. To that end, security cameras (“cameras”) are placed in selected areas of Library premises to discourage individuals from threatening the safety and/or security of Library patrons, staff, and/or property by enhancing its ability to identify and take the appropriate recourse against said individuals. 2. Applicability This policy applies to all Library premises and the individual, patrons, and staff therein or thereon. 3. Privacy Cameras may be placed in indoor and outdoor areas where individuals lack a reasonable expectation of privacy, such as entrances, delivery areas, parking lots, public seating areas, and service desks. Cameras will not be placed in areas where individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy, such as restrooms or private offices. Cameras are not positioned to identify a person’s individual habits or nonpublic activities. To the extent that any recorded images include identifiable persons providing or receiving confidential, sensitive, or private material, such records shall be treated as confidential to the extent required by Massachusetts law. 4. Public Notice Signs are posted at all public building entrances to notify the public and Library staff that security cameras are in use. Security cameras are not continuously monitored and both Library staff and members of the public should take appropriate precautions for their safety and the security of their personal property. The Library is not responsible for loss of or damage to personal property, nor is it responsible for personal injury; the Library assumes no duty by placing these security cameras on its premises. 5. Data Storage & Access Cameras record activities in real time. No audible communication shall be monitored or recorded by the security cameras. The length of time that recordings are stored varies depending on how much is recorded, but recordings are typically deleted after 14 days. When an incident occurs that may affect the safety and/or security of an individual located in or on Library premises or of any Library property, or when an incident occurs that may result in legal or administrative proceedings, the Library reserves the right to save the recordings thereof for as long as it deems necessary. The recorders and recorded data are located in a controlled area with limited access. Only the Library Director and designated Library or Town staff are authorized to access live and recorded video footage in pursuit of incidents of criminal activity or violations of Library policy. Other staff members may be given authorization to access or view footage on a limited basis. Information technology staff and facilities maintenance staff may also use and access Library security cameras to aid in the operation of Library facilities and ensure proper operation of the system, with reasonable notice to staff. Authorized individuals may use recorded data to identify those responsible for Library policy violations, criminal activity on Library property, or actions considered unreasonable and disruptive to normal Library operations. Authorized individuals may also request that law enforcement review a still image or selected portion of recorded data for assessing the security risk of a specific individual or for investigation of a crime on Library property. Recorded data may be shared with Library staff to identify individuals prohibited from entering upon Library premises and to maintain a safe, secure, and policy-compliant environment. Still images may remain posted in staff areas for the duration of the period for which an individual is prohibited from entering Library premises. The Library may use data collected through the cameras as part of a disciplinary investigation of its employees when the Library has reasonable suspicion of employee misconduct. The Library may also monitor and review security camera feeds and recordings as needed to support investigations and to enhance public safety, but the Library will not routinely monitored camera feeds in real time without cause. Qualifying incidents and/or bases for reasonable suspicion, include, but are not limited to: injury or accident, employee misconduct, employee and/or patron safety concerns, public safety, investigation related purposes, and property damage or loss. Footage will be kept on file for up to 30 days for investigation purposes and follow the protocols of the Commonwealth for retention of records. Once an investigation is completed, the Union will be informed of the exact reason that the footage has been reviewed. Employees are PROHIBITED from removing, damaging, altering or attempting to alter, disabling, or attempting to disable any camera on Library premises. 6. Law Enforcement Access To view live or recorded images from Library cameras, law enforcement must request permission to do so from the Library Director or his/her designee. Recorded data will be accorded the level of confidentiality and protection consistent with the Library’s obligations under Massachusetts’ Public Records and Privacy Laws. Access is also allowed by law enforcement when pursuant to a subpoena, court order, or when otherwise required by law. The Library reserves the right to consult legal counsel in matters involving warrants or subpoenas seeking access to security footage. Upon approval by the Library Director or his/her designee, recorded images may be shared voluntarily with law enforcement, unless the images are protected by Massachusetts law. If an emergency occurs on Library premises covered by security cameras that threatens the safety of the public, Library patrons, Library staff, or Library property, the Marblehead Police Department or other appropriate public safety/law enforcement authority, will be granted temporary access to the live camera feed in order to coordinate the emergency response. This access must be terminated once the situation is under control and the threat to the safety of the public, Library patrons, Library staff, or Library property has passed. Information obtained from camera footage will be used exclusively for the purposes set forth in this policy. The Library Director or his/her designee may authorize the review or release of camera footage for legitimate purposes, including the protection of patrons, staff, and property, and to protect the Library from exposure to liability. 7. Requests from the General Public Members of the public requesting to inspect security camera footage will be advised to file a police report or public records request, subject to applicable exemptions and protections. 8. Unauthorized Access and/or Disclosure All Library staff will be made aware of their obligations under federal and state law regarding protection of privacy. Failure to comply with this policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination from employment. Any employee who becomes aware of any unauthorized disclosure of a video record and/or a potential privacy breach has an affirmative obligation to immediately inform the Library Director or his/her designee of the same. 9. Disclaimer The Library disclaims any liability for use of the video data in accordance with the terms of this Policy, given that the Library is a public municipal entity within the Commonwealth, and the security cameras shall be limited to those areas where patrons and/or staff have no reasonable expectation of privacy. This Policy supersedes and overrides any other existing policy applicable to the use and operation of security cameras on Library property. A pproved by the Board of Trustees October, 2024. Social Media Policy Purpose and Mission Abbot Public Library’s mission is to serve as a vibrant hub of education, community and culture for the Town of Marblehead. It will continue to be a free, inclusive and safe gathering place for all where the pursuit of knowledge, literacy and continuing education is actively encouraged and supported. With this in consideration, Abbot Public Library maintains a Facebook and Instagram page to better serve our community by providing social media outlets for patrons to find information about the library’s programs, services, and resources. Our Social Media sites aim to serve as a means of communication between the library and library users as well as the general public. Please understand that, while our page is available at all times, to best utilize staff resources we are only able to monitor the site regularly during normal business hours. For questions about your library account, registering for programs, and other services with which you need direct staff assistance, please contact the library at 781-631-1481. This policy is adopted in addition to Abbot Public Library’s Internet Use Policy . Posting Guidelines Please be advised that Abbot Public Library’s Social Media is a moderated discussion, not a public forum. With that in mind, APL reserves the right to remove postings that we deem to be inappropriate. Inappropriate content includes, but is not limited to, the following: Content of a graphic nature. The use of vulgar language. Any post that discriminates against a person or a group because of race, age, gender, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, nationality, socioeconomic status, or religion. Commercial advertisements or any kind of solicitation, including those political in nature. Comments not relevant to the topic being discussed or to the APL in general. Personal attacks on other users, the library, or the library staff. Endorsements and/or inciting of illegal activity. Infringements of copyright or trademark. Renewal requests, museum pass requests, and hold requests. These should be done in person, over the phone, or via our email. Consequences Any post, comment, or message that fails to comply with Abbot Public Library’s policy as outlined above will be removed. Serious and repeat offenders may be banned from Abbot Public Library’s social media sites. Disclaimer Patrons sign up for and use Social Media sites at their own risk, and Abbot Public Library is not responsible for damages resulting from use of the sites. For your privacy and protection please remember that anything you post on APL’s page can and will be seen by others, not just library staff, and are considered to be public record. Do not post anything pertaining to your library account, including your card number, pin number, or contact information. Content posted by third parties on Abbot Public Library’s Social Media does not reflect the views or positions of the library, its staff, its volunteers, the Board of Trustees, or the Friends of Abbot Public Library. Please also be aware that the social media sites used by Abbot Public Library have their own privacy policies and Terms of Use. Approved by the Board of Trustees, April 2023 Use of Gallery Space Policy POLICY FOR THE USE OF GALLERY SPACE In accordance with the Library’s role as an informational, educational or cultural center, Abbot Public Library (APL) offers exhibit spaces at 235 Pleasant Street. The Library endeavors to present a variety of art, art forms and artists. THE VIRGINIA A. CARTEN GALLERY Virginia A. Carten was a local artist who expressed her generosity to the community by bequeathing a significant amount of money to APL for items relating to art and artists. A portion of that money was used to build a new galley during the building renovations in 1989-1990. The Virginia A. Carten Gallery is used for exhibits of area artists whose work might include painting, photography, sculpture, multimedia and more. Potential exhibitors must submit an application form. By submitting an application, artists agree to follow the guidelines in this policy. Applications will be reviewed on a regular basis. Scheduling will be at the discretion of the Library Director, taking into consideration other activities that may be taking place in the library. Additional guidelines: Exhibits are generally scheduled for about six weeks, but may run for a different time period, to be determined by the Library Director. Regarding selection of exhibits, APL reserves the right to refuse or remove material deemed unsuitable. This could include the following conditions: items in the exhibit are a possible safety hazard, inappropriate for the display space, interfere with the use of library facilities, impede library staff in the performance of their duties, or endanger the library building or collection. The artwork must be appropriate for viewing by the community, and selection is at the sole discretion of APL. Exhibitors are responsible for both setting up and removing their display during regular business hours. Artwork must be suitably framed and wired and must be hung on the library’s preinstalled picture rail. Nothing is to be affixed to the walls. Exhibits must be removed by the exhibitor on the last day of the exhibit period. APL does not provide storage space. Price tags are not to be displayed on the art works. A handout listing prices may be made available by the artist with the artist’s contact info. All sales transactions must take place off Library premises. APL will not engage in selling or negotiating for the sale of artwork. Exhibits are open to the public during regular library hours. Receptions may be scheduled by the APL and are subject to the terms and conditions outlined in the Library’s Meeting Room policy. MARBLEHEAD ARTS ASSOCIATION WALL The Library is partnering with Marblehead Arts Association (MAA) to showcase a rotation of exhibits of local artists. Selection and scheduling arrangements will be at the discretion of MAA. DONATIONS OR LOANS OF ART Art donations or loans will be accepted at the discretion of the Library Director. LIABILITY APL cannot assume liability for lost, stolen, or damaged art. Exhibit space is unsecured and unsupervised. In the application, each potential exhibitor will be asked to agree to a waiver holding APL, its officials and employees, and the Town of Marblehead harmless for lost or damaged art. Exhibitors will be held financially liable for any damage caused to the library walls or property during the hanging process. Through the Town of Marblehead, each exhibit is insured for a maximum of $25,000 for the entire exhibit and up to $2,500 for any individual piece. There is a $250 deductible, payable by the artist. The artist will supply the Library Director with a list of the individual works and their values. SALES Any sales must be arranged directly between the potential buyer and the artist. The Library staff is not involved in any part of this process; they do not collect, hold or keep any funds in connection with an exhibit or possible sale. The Library will include gallery events in its monthly publicity efforts (subject to monthly publicity deadlines). Artists will be asked for a description of their work and accompanying images. Reviewed and approved by the Board of Trustees, November 2022; Revised, October 2024. Volunteer Policy Abbot Public Library volunteers supplement the efforts of paid Library staff to provide quality Library services and programs. Volunteering at Abbot Public Library also serves as a method for area residents to become familiar with the Library and creates opportunities for individuals to serve the community. Volunteers are liaisons to the community and, by their contribution, are advocates for quality library service. Policy specifics are: 1. Volunteers perform tasks for the Abbot Public Library without wages, benefits, or compensation of any kind. They are service volunteers who do work for the Library and function in ways similar to staff but do not replace staff. 2. Volunteers are recognized by the public as representatives of the Library and shall be guided by the same work and behavior codes as employees. They work with the status similar to “at-will” employees. Volunteers agree that the Library may at any time and for any reason decide to terminate the relationship or change the assignment. 3. Youth 13 and up may serve as volunteers with the written consent of a parent or legal guardian. 4. Volunteers are bound by Abbot Public Library’s Policies as well as the rules for privacy and confidentiality in accordance with the Library’s standard policies, adherence to NOBLE policy and the American Library Association Bill of Rights and Freedom to Read Statement . 5. All volunteer tasks must aid the Library in achieving its goals and objectives as specified in the Strategic Plan and be consistent with the purpose and intent of the volunteer program. Volunteer talents, experience and interests will be considered. 6. Volunteers shall work under the supervision of a Library staff member. 7. Volunteers are expected to contribute positively to a work environment that is free from unwelcome overtures, advances or coercion by or to any volunteer, employee, or member of the public using Library facilities or attending a Library program. 8. Volunteers will be asked to sign an agreement which specifies expectations and guidelines. Reviewed and approved by the Board of Trustees, November 2022. Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum The Gardner Museum houses the collection of Isabella Stewart Gardner, a local patron of the arts who died in 1924, in a building she commissioned to look like a 15th century Venetian palazzo. The center of the original building showcases courtyard plantings that reflect the seasons as they evolve throughout the year. In addition to her collection, which remains displayed exactly as she arranged it per her will, there are changing exhibitions, concerts, lectures, family events, and an artist-in-residence program. In 2012, the museum opened an addition designed by Renzo Piano that houses a space for their concert series.
